perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York without regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California with regard to its conflicts of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to California, and explicitly stating 'with regard to its conflicts of laws provisions', introduces uncertainty. New York and California have different conflict of laws rules. By stating that conflict of laws principles should be considered, the contract could lead to different interpretations, because the NDA is being entered in New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law to govern contracts involving at least $250,000, regardless of conflict of law principles. The modified text contradicts this by applying California conflict of laws principles, which might direct the application of another state's laws despite the parties' intent to be governed by New York law, creating uncertainty and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 (2022) - Choice of Law. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 5 - Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations Title 14 - Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum in Certain Transactions 5-1401 - Choice of Law. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. New York may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from New York to California and includes conflicts of law provisions. The Law Explanation states that New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law for contracts over $250,000, regardless of conflict of law principles. The changed text contradicts this by applying California conflict of laws principles. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by stating that New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 addresses the 'Choice of Law'. The original text adheres to this by selecting New York law. The modified text introduces California law and its conflict of laws principles, potentially undermining the initial choice of New York law, which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall not disclose any Sample Data to any third party or to its own employees, agents or professional advisors except employees, agents or professional advisors who are required to have the Sample Data to implement the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall not disclose any Sample Data to any third party or to its own employees, agents or professional advisors, regardless of their need to implement the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for employees, agents, or professional advisors 'required to have the Sample Data to implement the Permitted Purpose,' the contract becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable. It contradicts the concept of reasonable access and legitimate business need, potentially stifling internal use of the data in a way that conflicts with trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by California)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text overly restricts access even within the company, which could be interpreted as unreasonable restraint of trade and conflict with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by California). The UTSA protects trade secrets but doesn't prevent internal use by employees who need the information for their job. By disallowing any disclosure, the contract could prevent the developer from implementing safety procedures by telling people who should know, conflicting with the UTSA's provisions about reasonable use and protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.location: 1. Non-Disclosure and Non-Use of Sample Data b. Non-Disclosure

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the exception that allows the developer to disclose sample data to its own employees, agents, or professional advisors when 'required to have the Sample Data to implement the Permitted Purpose.' This change creates an overly broad restriction, potentially conflicting with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as adopted in California, which allows for reasonable internal use of trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the modified text 'overly restricts access even within the company, which could be interpreted as unreasonable restraint of trade'. Furthermore, California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent,' and the modified text could be interpreted as removing implied consent for necessary internal use.

perturbation.original_text: When the Assessment Period is over, if the parties have not agreed to go forward and sign a comprehensive agreement that permits Developer to use the Data to modify its Applications and pursuant to which FDI will provide Developer with the technical documents that are necessary for Developer to do so, Developer shall return to FDI or destroy all copies of the Sample Data in its possession or control.
perturbation.changed_text: When the Assessment Period is over, if the parties have not agreed to go forward and sign a comprehensive agreement that permits Developer to use the Data to modify its Applications and pursuant to which FDI will provide Developer with the technical documents that are necessary for Developer to do so, Developer may retain all copies of the Sample Data in its possession or control for archival purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall return to FDI or destroy' to 'may retain... for archival purposes' directly contradicts the original agreement's intent to protect the sample data and constitutes unauthorized possession of trade secrets after the agreement has expired.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Retaining the data after the agreement ends and without permission constitutes misappropriation, which is a violation of the DTSA. This modification enables continued possession, which can be seen as a violation of rights granted by the DTSA, as defined in 18 U.S. Code § 1839(5).

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement and creates a legal contradiction concerning trade secret misappropriation under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The original text mandates the return or destruction of sample data after the assessment period, while the changed text allows for retaining the data 'for archival purposes.' This retention, without explicit permission, conflicts with the protections afforded to trade secrets as it constitutes unauthorized possession. Law Snippet 1 references the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, and Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1836, which concerns civil proceedings related to violations of the DTSA. The change from 'shall return or destroy' to 'may retain' fundamentally alters the obligation regarding the data, increasing the risk of misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Developer agrees that money damages would not be an adequate remedy for unauthorized use or disclosure by it of any Sample Data, and that, in addition to all other remedies, Developer shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for Developer’s unauthorized use or disclosure, without the requirement of posting any bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer agrees that money damages would be an adequate remedy for unauthorized use or disclosure by it of any Sample Data, and that, in addition to all other remedies, Developer may not seek specific performance or injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for Developer’s unauthorized use or disclosure, with the requirement of posting a bond.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the statement regarding the adequacy of money damages and the availability of injunctive relief significantly alters the remedies available to FDI in case of a breach. By stating money damages are adequate, and preventing injunctive relief while mandating a bond, the contract weakens FDI's ability to protect its trade secrets effectively.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.2 allows for injunctive relief to prevent threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text attempts to contractually limit this remedy, making it harder for the trade secret owner to obtain immediate protection, thus potentially violating the principles and remedies afforded under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3426.2, which allows for injunctive relief to prevent threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text acknowledged the inadequacy of money damages and the availability of injunctive relief. The changed text reverses this, stating money damages are adequate and preventing injunctive relief. This modification weakens the trade secret owner's ability to obtain immediate protection as the right to injunctive relief is now removed, thus contradicting the remedy afforded under CUTSA as supported in the law snippet: 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.'
